1:
On Deuteronomy 11:1 — “Therefore thou shalt love the LORD thy God, and keep his charge, and his statutes, and his judgments, and his commandments, alway” — the witnesses mark the call to love and obey God.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: there is no break here — “the LORD hath made thee as the stars of heaven for multitude, and thou shalt love the LORD”; “keep his charge,” literally “keep his keeping,” all that is to be kept. The Puritan Henry: because God has built them into families and multiplied them as the stars, they must love him. The Reformed Calvin: the whole address aims that the people testify their gratitude by obedience; he requires love before all.

The verse sets the duty. Love the LORD and keep his commandments always. Gratitude is to issue in love and obedience.

So the witnesses see the chapter open with the great duty of loving God, flowing from his goodness, and issuing in obedience. The witnesses note the connection Ellicott and Henry draw to the close of the previous chapter: God had made Israel as the stars of heaven for multitude, building a small beginning into a great people; “therefore thou shalt love the LORD thy God” — the love is the response of gratitude to so great a Benefactor. They mark Calvin’s observation that the whole address has this scope: that the people, allured by God’s bounties, should testify their gratitude by their obedience and reverently embrace his law; and that Moses requires love before and above all other duties, for love is the spring of true obedience. They observe the comprehensiveness of the charge: “keep his charge, and his statutes, and his judgments, and his commandments” — Ellicott notes the phrase “keep his keeping,” that is, observe all that God has given to be observed; the whole law, in all its parts, is to be kept. They mark the constancy required: “alway” — not by fits or for a season, but continually, all their days; love and obedience are to be the steady tenor of the life. They note the order the witnesses see: that love comes first, and obedience flows from it; the keeping of the commandments is the fruit and proof of love to God (John 14:15). They observe that God’s goodness is the ground of the duty, and love its spring. The verse thus sets the duty of love and obedience — grounded, in the next verses, on God’s great acts.
2:
On Deuteronomy 11:2 — “And know ye this day: for I speak not with your children which have not known… the chastisement of the LORD your God, his greatness, his mighty hand” — the witnesses mark the present generation as eyewitnesses of God’s acts.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: to awaken this love, the people are reminded of God’s mighty acts which they had seen. Charles Ellicott: those less than twenty at the Exodus would still be living, and the events of their youth must be remembered. The Reformed Calvin: they had been more than sufficiently taught by God’s illustrious acts. Wesley: “know,” acknowledge and consider with diligence and thankfulness.

The verse appeals to their own experience. They, not their children, have known God’s chastisement and might. The witnesses are addressed.

So the witnesses see Moses ground the call to love and obey God on the people’s own experience of his mighty acts. The witnesses note the appeal to their knowledge: “know ye this day” — Wesley explains this not of a bare knowing, but of acknowledging and considering with diligence and thankfulness what God had done; the people were to lay it to heart. They mark the distinction Moses draws: “I speak not with your children which have not known” — he addresses, Ellicott observes, those who had themselves seen God’s works, for all who were under twenty at the Exodus would still be living and must remember the events of their youth; the appeal is to eyewitnesses, not to a later generation who knew the acts only by report. They observe what they had witnessed: “the chastisement of the LORD your God, his greatness, his mighty hand, and his stretched out arm” — both the corrections God had laid on his people and the mighty wonders he had wrought; they had seen his power and his discipline alike. They note the purpose Keil and Delitzsch draw out: that the remembrance of these things was to awaken and strengthen their love to God; what they had seen with their own eyes should bind their hearts to him. They observe Calvin’s point that the people had been more than sufficiently taught by God’s illustrious acts how great was his power and how remarkable his mercy toward them. They note that experience of God lays an obligation of love and obedience. The verse thus appeals to their own witness — the acts rehearsed in the next verses.
3:
On Deuteronomy 11:3 — “And his miracles, and his acts, which he did in the midst of Egypt unto Pharaoh the king of Egypt, and unto all his land” — the witnesses mark the wonders done in Egypt.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the miracles and acts done in the midst of Egypt unto Pharaoh and all his land. The Reformed Gill: the miraculous works done there, the ten plagues inflicted on the Egyptians for refusing to let Israel go.

The verse recalls Egypt. God’s miracles and acts upon Pharaoh and his land are remembered. The wonders of the Exodus are set before them.

So the witnesses see Moses recall the great wonders God had done in Egypt, which the people had seen. The witnesses note the works named: “his miracles, and his acts, which he did in the midst of Egypt” — Gill identifies these as the miraculous works done in Egypt, the ten plagues inflicted on the Egyptians for their refusing to let Israel go; the mighty signs by which God broke the power of Egypt and brought his people out. They mark the objects of the judgments: “unto Pharaoh the king of Egypt, and unto all his land” — the plagues fell on the king who had oppressed Israel and on his whole land; God dealt with the proudest of kings and the mightiest of nations, and overcame them. They observe the force of the remembrance the witnesses see: that the people had themselves seen these things, the river turned to blood, the darkness, the death of the firstborn, and all the wonders of God’s power; the memory of them was to bind their hearts to the God who had so mightily delivered them. They note that the God who claimed their love and obedience was the God who had judged Egypt for their sake; his power and his care for them were proved beyond all doubt. They observe that the rehearsing of God’s acts is a chief means to stir up love and obedience; the people are to consider what God has done. They note that the wonders of the Exodus were the foundation of Israel’s faith. The verse thus recalls the wonders in Egypt — the overthrow at the Red Sea following in the next verse.
4:
On Deuteronomy 11:4 — “And what he did unto the army of Egypt, unto their horses, and to their chariots; how he made the water of the Red sea to overflow them… and how the LORD hath destroyed them unto this day” — the witnesses mark the overthrow at the Red Sea.

The Reformed Gill: what God did to the numerous army of Egypt at the Red sea, when they pursued Israel to bring them back or destroy them. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “unto this day” — the effect of that destruction continues, in Egypt’s weakness and fear, and Israel’s safety from their further attempts.

The verse recalls the Red Sea. God drowned Egypt’s army, horses, and chariots in the sea. The great deliverance is remembered.

So the witnesses see Moses recall the destruction of Egypt’s army in the Red Sea, the crowning act of the deliverance. The witnesses note the overthrow: “how he made the water of the Red sea to overflow them, as they pursued after you” — Gill observes that the numerous army of Egypt, with their horses and chariots, pursued Israel to the sea to bring them back or destroy them, and God made the waters return upon them and drown them; the host that had hunted Israel perished in the deep (Exodus 14:28). They mark the completeness of it: “the LORD hath destroyed them unto this day” — Wesley draws out that the effect of that destruction continued to the present, in the lasting weakness and fear of Egypt and the safety of Israel from any further attempt of theirs; the blow at the Red Sea broke Egypt’s power against them for good. They observe the force of the remembrance: that the people had seen with their own eyes the proud army of their oppressors swallowed by the sea, while they passed through on dry ground; no deliverance could be more signal, none more fit to bind their hearts to God. They note that the God who claimed their love had not only smitten Egypt with plagues but utterly destroyed their pursuing host; his salvation was complete. They observe that the memory of so great a deliverance should have made Israel forever faithful. They note that the destruction of the enemy was the salvation of the people. The verse thus recalls the Red Sea — the wilderness dealings following in the next verses.
5:
On Deuteronomy 11:5 — “And what he did unto you in the wilderness, until ye came unto this place” — the witnesses mark God’s dealings in the wilderness.

The Reformed Gill: meaning not so much the good things done in divers places, as the chastisements and corrections God exercised them with for their murmurings and rebellions. Calvin: Moses sets before them, by way of summary, God’s acts, to heighten the authority of the Law.

The verse recalls the wilderness. God’s dealings with them all the way to this place are remembered. His discipline as well as his care is owned.

So the witnesses see Moses recall God’s dealings with Israel throughout the wilderness journey, down to their present place. The witnesses note the scope: “what he did unto you in the wilderness, until ye came unto this place” — the whole long history of God’s working with them through the forty years, from the Red Sea to the plains of Moab. They mark Gill’s observation of what is chiefly meant: not so much the good things God did for them in various places — the manna, the water, the guidance — as the chastisements and corrections he exercised them with for their murmurings, rebellions, and unbelief; the wilderness was a place of discipline as well as of provision. They observe Calvin’s note that Moses here sets before the people, in a brief summary, the acts of God, to heighten the authority of the Law and to bind them to obedience; the rehearsal of God’s dealings is a call to reverence his word. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God’s chastisements, no less than his mercies, were tokens of his care, meant to humble, to prove, and to correct his people; they had seen his hand both in blessing and in discipline, and both should teach them to fear and love him. They observe that the whole wilderness way bore witness to God’s dealing with them as a father deals with his children (Deuteronomy 8:5). They note that God’s corrections are part of his love. The verse thus recalls the wilderness — the judgment on Dathan and Abiram following in the next verse.
6:
On Deuteronomy 11:6 — “And what he did unto Dathan and Abiram… how the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up… in the midst of all Israel” — the witnesses mark the judgment on Dathan and Abiram.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: it is impossible to separate the rebellion of Korah from that of Dathan and Abiram (Numbers 16:1); the whole point of Korah’s rebellion was the priesthood. The Reformed Gill: when they with Korah quarrelled with Moses and Aaron, the earth opened and swallowed them.

The verse recalls the rebels’ doom. The earth swallowed Dathan and Abiram before all Israel. God’s judgment on rebellion is remembered.

So the witnesses see Moses recall the awful judgment on Dathan and Abiram, swallowed alive by the earth, as a warning. The witnesses note the doom: “the earth opened her mouth, and swallowed them up, and their households, and their tents” — Gill recalls that these Reubenites, with Korah, had risen against Moses and Aaron, quarrelling against God’s appointed authority; and the earth opened and swallowed them up alive, with all that pertained to them (Numbers 16:31). They mark the public character of the judgment: “in the midst of all Israel” — it was done openly, before the eyes of the whole congregation, that all might see and fear; the people had witnessed this terrible act of God against rebellion. They observe the point Ellicott raises, that the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram cannot really be separated from that of Korah, whose whole quarrel was over the priesthood; though here only the Reubenites are named (who were swallowed by the earth, while Korah’s company perished by fire), the rebellion was one. They note the lesson the witnesses draw from the recalling of it: that the people had seen with their own eyes how God punishes those who rise against his ordinance and authority; the memory of so dreadful a judgment should keep them from the like rebellion and bind them to obedience. They observe that God’s severity against the rebels was a warning to all. They note that to resist God’s appointed order is to court his judgment. The verse thus recalls the rebels’ doom — the appeal to their own sight following in the next verse.
7:
On Deuteronomy 11:7 — “But your eyes have seen all the great acts of the LORD which he did” — the witnesses mark the people as eyewitnesses.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, “your eyes are the witnesses (the seers) of all the great working of Jehovah which he hath wrought.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: all had seen some, and some all, of the great things in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness; what our eyes have seen, especially in early days, should be improved by us.

The verse presses their witness. Their own eyes have seen God’s great acts. The appeal is to their sight.

So the witnesses see Moses drive home the appeal: the people had seen all these great acts of God with their own eyes. The witnesses note the force of the phrase: “your eyes have seen all the great acts of the LORD” — Ellicott renders it “your eyes are the witnesses, the seers, of all the great working of Jehovah”; the people were not hearers only, but eyewitnesses of God’s mighty deeds; they could not plead ignorance, for they had seen. They mark Wesley’s note that all of them had seen some, and some of them all, of the great things done in Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness; the whole generation was a body of witnesses to God’s power and care. They observe the obligation the witnesses see in this: that what a man has seen with his own eyes binds him more than what he has only heard; the people who had beheld God’s acts were the more bound to love and obey him, and the less excusable if they rebelled. They note Wesley’s practical lesson: that what our eyes have seen, especially in our early days, should be improved by us — the mercies and judgments of God witnessed in youth should be laid to heart and made the ground of lifelong obedience. They observe that God appeals to experience, the surest of teachers; the people knew his greatness not by report but by sight. They note that seen mercies lay the deepest obligation. The verse thus presses their witness — the call to keep the commandments following in the next verses.
8:
On Deuteronomy 11:8 — “Therefore shall ye keep all the commandments which I command you this day, that ye may be strong, and go in and possess the land” — the witnesses mark obedience the way to strength and possession.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: keep the commandments, because God can execute such terrible judgments and has given such proofs of his power. Charles Ellicott: “the commandment” (singular) — one course of action; “go in and possess,” complete the conquest so as to enjoy the whole land. The Puritan Henry: still Moses urges the same, loth to conclude; keep the commandments, and enter the good land.

The verse joins obedience and possession. Keep the commandments, that you may be strong and possess the land. Obedience is the way to the inheritance.

So the witnesses see Moses press, on the ground of all God’s acts, the keeping of the commandments as the way to strength and to the possession of the land. The witnesses note the inference: “therefore shall ye keep all the commandments” — Clarke draws it out, that because God can execute such terrible judgments (as on Dathan and Abiram) and has given such proofs of his power and justice, the people should keep his commandments, lest they provoke the like against themselves. They mark the reward held out: “that ye may be strong, and go in and possess the land” — obedience would make them strong for the conquest and bring them into the inheritance; Ellicott notes that “go in and possess” means to complete the conquest in detail, so as to enjoy the whole profit of the land. They observe Ellicott’s point that “the commandment” stands in the singular, marking one settled course of obedient action rather than a heap of separate details; the law is one way to be walked in. They note Henry’s observation that Moses, loth to conclude, still urges the same point, that keeping the commandments is the way to enter and enjoy the good land — a type, he adds, of the life to come; if thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments. They observe the principle the witnesses see: that strength for duty and enjoyment of God’s gifts go with obedience; the obedient are made strong, and possess their inheritance. They note that the way to the promised land lay through the keeping of God’s law. The verse thus joins obedience and possession — the promise of long days following in the next verse.
9:
On Deuteronomy 11:9 — “And that ye may prolong your days in the land, which the LORD sware unto your fathers… a land that floweth with milk and honey” — the witnesses mark the promise of long days in the good land.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: that ye may prolong your days in the land which the LORD sware unto your fathers, a land flowing with milk and honey. The Reformed Gill: not only enter and possess, but continue long, which depended on their obedience.

The verse adds the promise of continuance. Obedience prolongs their days in the good land. Long life in the inheritance is the reward.

So the witnesses see the reward of obedience enlarged: not only to enter and possess the land, but to prolong their days in it. The witnesses note the further promise: “that ye may prolong your days in the land” — Gill observes that obedience would secure not merely the taking of the land, but the long continuance of the people in it; their abiding in the inheritance depended on their keeping the law. They mark the land’s description: “a land that floweth with milk and honey” — the well-known token of its richness and plenty, a land of abundance and delight; God gave his people no barren or grudging country, but a good and fruitful one. They observe the ground of the gift: “which the LORD sware unto your fathers to give unto them and to their seed” — the land was theirs by God’s oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, a sure inheritance founded on the divine promise. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that obedience is the way not only to obtain God’s blessings but to keep and enjoy them long; the people would hold the land as long as they held to God, and lose it when they forsook him — as their later history showed. They observe that length of days in the good land was a real and great reward, the token of God’s continued favour. They note that the enjoyment of God’s gifts is bound to obedience. The verse thus adds the promise of long days — the nature of the land unfolded in the next verses.
10:
On Deuteronomy 11:10 — “For the land, whither thou goest in to possess it, is not as the land of Egypt… where thou sowedst thy seed, and wateredst it with thy foot, as a garden of herbs” — the witnesses mark the contrast with Egypt’s watering by labour.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: rain scarcely ever falls in Egypt; God supplies it by the inundation of the Nile, water collected in ponds and directed by channels. Charles Ellicott: not as Egypt — but better; Egypt was praised above all lands (Genesis 13:10). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “with thy foot” — with great pains, fetching and dispersing water, digging furrows, and using engines worked by the foot.

The verse contrasts the lands. Egypt was watered by the foot’s labour; Canaan is not so. The toil of Egypt is set against the gift of rain.

So the witnesses see Moses contrast the promised land with Egypt in the matter of its watering. The witnesses note the manner of Egypt’s watering: “where thou sowedst thy seed, and wateredst it with thy foot, as a garden of herbs” — Clarke explains that in Egypt rain scarcely ever falls, and God supplies its lack by the yearly inundation of the Nile; and where the flood does not reach, water is collected in ponds and channels and brought to the fields by labour. They mark Wesley’s account of “watering with the foot”: it was done with great pains and toil of the feet — by going up and down to fetch and disperse the water, by digging furrows, and by working the engines and treadwheels that raised and distributed the Nile’s water; the Egyptian husbandman watered his ground as a man waters a garden of herbs, by his own ceaseless labour. They observe the point of the contrast, which the witnesses draw out: the land of Canaan was not like Egypt, dependent on the river and on man’s toil to water it, but (the next verse shows) a land that drank the rain of heaven, watered directly by God’s hand. They note Ellicott’s observation that, though Egypt was praised above all lands for its fertility (Genesis 13:10), Canaan was better still, for it depended not on the river but on God. They observe the lesson the witnesses see: that Egypt’s watering was by labour and the eye to the river, but Canaan’s by faith and the eye to heaven. They note that the contrast teaches dependence on God. The verse thus contrasts the lands — Canaan’s rain from heaven following in the next verse.
11:
On Deuteronomy 11:11 — “But the land, whither ye go to possess it, is a land of hills and valleys, and drinketh water of the rain of heaven” — the witnesses mark Canaan, the land that drinks rain from heaven.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: as the Jewish commentator prettily says, “while thou sleepest on thy bed, the Holy One waters it high and low” (cf. the parable in Mark 4:26). The Reformed Gill: a land of hills and valleys, not to be watered as Egypt by a river and canals. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: of hills and valleys, and so more healthful than Egypt, which the Nile’s overflow made unpleasant.

The verse describes Canaan. A land of hills and valleys, drinking the rain of heaven. God himself waters it.

So the witnesses see the promised land described as one watered by the rain of heaven, by God’s own hand. The witnesses note its character: “a land of hills and valleys” — Gill observes that, being hilly and uneven, it could not be watered as Egypt was, by the overflow of a river and the cutting of canals across a flat plain; Wesley adds that this made it more healthful than Egypt, which the Nile’s yearly overflow rendered unpleasant and unwholesome. They mark the great difference: “drinketh water of the rain of heaven” — the land was watered, not by the labour of men nor by a river, but directly by the rain that God sent from above; Ellicott quotes the lovely saying of the Jewish commentator, “while thou sleepest on thy bed, the Holy One waters it high and low,” comparing it to the parable of the seed growing the husbandman knows not how (Mark 4:26–27). They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that Canaan was a land kept in immediate dependence on God; whereas Egypt looked to its river and its own toil, Israel must look up to heaven for the rain that made their land fruitful; their harvests hung on God’s blessing, not on their engines. They note the spiritual fitness of this: that God so ordered the land of his people that they should be ever cast upon him, their eyes lifted to heaven for their daily bread; the want or the gift of rain would teach them their dependence. They observe that the land that drinks the rain of heaven is the emblem of a people who live by faith on God. They note that God himself was Israel’s husbandman. The verse thus describes Canaan — God’s care over it following in the next verse.
12:
On Deuteronomy 11:12 — “A land which the LORD thy God careth for: the eyes of the LORD thy God are always upon it, from the beginning of the year even unto the end of the year” — the witnesses mark God’s constant care over the land.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, “seeketh”; compare “a land that I had espied for them… the glory of all lands” (Ezekiel 20:6). The Reformed Gill: God cares for it in a special manner, beyond his general care of the world. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: watering it as it were by his own hand, without man’s help, and giving peculiar blessings to it.

The verse marks God’s care. The LORD cares for the land, his eyes always upon it. The land is under his special providence.

So the witnesses see the promised land marked out as one under God’s special and constant care. The witnesses note the tender word: “a land which the LORD thy God careth for” — Ellicott observes the Hebrew is “seeketh” or “searcheth out,” as of a land God had espied and chosen for his people, the glory of all lands (Ezekiel 20:6); Gill marks that, while God has a general providence over the whole earth, he cares for this land in a special and peculiar manner. They mark the constancy of his care: “the eyes of the LORD thy God are always upon it, from the beginning of the year even unto the end of the year” — God’s watchful eye rests on the land continually, all the year round, ordering its seasons, its rains, and its fruitfulness; his care never sleeps nor turns away. They observe Wesley’s point that God watered this land as it were by his own hand, without man’s help, and gave it peculiar blessings that Egypt and other lands enjoyed not; it was a land singularly favoured. They note the comfort and the warning the witnesses see in this: that the land’s fruitfulness hung on God’s care, so that his people, dwelling there, were ever in his sight and under his hand; his favour would make it flourish, his displeasure (the next verses show) would make it barren. They observe the typology some draw: that as God’s eyes are always on the land, so his eye is ever on his church and people, caring for them continually (1 Peter 3:12). They note that to dwell under God’s constant eye is both a mercy and a charge. The verse thus marks God’s care — the rain promised to obedience in the next verses.
13:
On Deuteronomy 11:13 — “And it shall come to pass, if ye shall hearken diligently unto my commandments… to love the LORD your God, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul” — the witnesses mark the condition of love and whole-hearted service.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: if ye hearken to love the LORD and serve him with all your heart and soul. Charles Ellicott: here begins the formal sanction of the charge by a declaration of rewards and punishments, a characteristic feature of the Law. The Reformed Gill: commanded in the name and by the authority of the Lord, the only lawgiver.

The verse states the condition. If they hearken, loving and serving God wholly, blessing follows. Whole-hearted love is the term of the promise.

So the witnesses see the great promise of fruitful seasons hung upon the condition of loving and serving God with the whole heart. The witnesses note the term: “if ye shall hearken diligently unto my commandments… to love the LORD your God, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul” — the blessing to follow is conditioned on a diligent, hearty obedience that springs from love; not a cold or partial keeping, but the service of the whole soul. They mark Ellicott’s observation that here begins the formal sanction of the charge, the declaration of the rewards of obedience and the punishments of disobedience, which (he notes) is a characteristic feature of the Law; the covenant sets life and death, blessing and curse, before the people. They observe Gill’s note that these commandments were given in the name and by the authority of the Lord, the only lawgiver; the obedience required is to God himself, not to man. They note the inwardness of the service demanded: “with all your heart and with all your soul” — the whole inner man, the affections and the will, is to be engaged; God will be served not with the hands only but with the heart, and that wholly, not in part. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the love which God requires is the spring of the obedience he rewards; where the heart is given, the service follows, and the blessing with it. They note that the gospel too binds love and obedience together (Matthew 22:37). They observe that whole-hearted love is the root of all acceptable service. The verse thus states the condition — the promise of rain following in the next verses.
14:
On Deuteronomy 11:14 — “That I will give you the rain of your land in his due season, the first rain and the latter rain, that thou mayest gather in thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil” — the witnesses mark the promise of seasonable rain.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the former rain fell about November, when they sowed, to prepare the ground; the latter before harvest. Charles Ellicott: the first rain after sowing, the latter just before harvest, in the ninth and first months (Joel 2:23). The Reformed Gill and Keil and Delitzsch: a sufficient quantity in proper time, that the land be fruitful.

The verse promises rain in season. The former and latter rain, that they may gather corn, wine, and oil. Fruitful harvests reward obedience.

So the witnesses see the first reward of obedience: God will give the rain of the land in its due season, that the harvests may be gathered. The witnesses note the promise: “I will give you the rain of your land in his due season” — Gill and Keil observe that God would give a sufficient quantity of rain, and that at the proper times, to make the land fruitful; the very rain on which the land depended (vv. 11–12) God himself would send to the obedient. They mark the two rains named: “the first rain and the latter rain” — Clarke and Ellicott explain these as the former or early rain, which fell about November when the seed was sown, to moisten and prepare the ground; and the latter or spring rain, which fell just before harvest, to swell and ripen the grain; both were needed, and both God promised in their season (Joel 2:23; James 5:7). They observe the fruit of this: “that thou mayest gather in thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil” — the three staples of the land, bread, wine, and oil, would be brought in abundantly when God gave the rain; plenty would crown their obedience. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that the seasonable rain, on which all their increase hung, was God’s gift to the obedient; their harvests were the reward of their faithfulness, and the proof of his care. They observe that God ties the blessings of nature to the obedience of his people; godliness has the promise of the life that now is (1 Timothy 4:8). They note that God’s gifts come in their due season. The verse thus promises seasonable rain — grass and fulness following in the next verse.
15:
On Deuteronomy 11:15 — “And I will send grass in thy fields for thy cattle, that thou mayest eat and be full” — the witnesses mark the promise of plenty and fulness.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: a further blessing, belonging to the food itself in man’s inward parts — for it is possible to eat and not be satisfied. The Reformed Gill: grass for cattle, by plentiful showers at proper times. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: godliness has the promise of the life that now is; yet the favour of God puts more gladness in the heart than the increase of corn, wine, and oil.

The verse promises plenty. Grass for the cattle, food for man, and fulness. Abundance and satisfaction reward obedience.

So the witnesses see the promise of plenty extended to the cattle and to the satisfying of the people. The witnesses note the provision for the beasts: “I will send grass in thy fields for thy cattle” — Gill observes that by the plentiful showers given at proper times, God would cause the grass to spring up, that there might be food for the cattle of every sort; the rain that fed the corn fed also the herds. They mark the provision for man, and the deeper blessing in it: “that thou mayest eat and be full” — Ellicott draws out a further mercy, that belongs not only to the abundance of food but to the food itself in man’s inward parts; for it is possible to eat and not be satisfied, but God promised both plenty and the satisfying of it — that his people should eat and be full, their hunger truly met. They observe Wesley’s balanced note: that godliness has here the promise of the life that now is, the obedient being blessed with earthly plenty; yet he adds that the favour of God puts more gladness into the heart than the increase of corn, wine, and oil (Psalm 4:7); the best of the blessing is God himself. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God gives his obedient people not bare provision but plenty, and not plenty only but contentment in it; he satisfies the desire of every living thing. They observe that to eat and be full is a token of God’s favour, but his favour itself is the greater good. They note that God provides for beast and man alike. The verse thus promises plenty and fulness — the warning against apostasy following in the next verses.
16:
On Deuteronomy 11:16 — “Take heed to yourselves, that your heart be not deceived, and ye turn aside, and serve other gods, and worship them” — the witnesses mark the warning against the deceived heart and idolatry.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: take heed when you are satisfied (cf. Deuteronomy 8:10). The Reformed Gill: lest the heart be deceived by the influence of the heavens on the earth, drawn to worship the host of heaven. Calvin: by often inculcating it, he arraigns man’s proneness to superstition; “that your heart be not deceived” — for by them men are deluded.

The verse warns of the deceived heart. Beware lest, turned aside, you serve other gods. Plenty must not breed apostasy.

So the witnesses see the promise of plenty followed at once by a solemn warning against the heart being deceived into idolatry. The witnesses note the caution: “take heed to yourselves, that your heart be not deceived” — Ellicott observes (comparing Deuteronomy 8:10) that the warning bears especially on the time when they are full and satisfied; for prosperity is a snare, and the well-fed heart is apt to forget God. They mark the particular danger Gill names: that the heart might be deceived by observing the influence of the heavens — the sun, moon, and stars — upon the fruitfulness of the earth, and so be drawn to worship the host of heaven, ascribing to the creature the gifts of the Creator; or be drawn away by the example of the idolatrous nations. They observe Calvin’s point that Moses, by often inculcating this caution, indirectly arraigns man’s proneness to superstition; the very phrase “that your heart be not deceived” owns how easily men are deluded into idolatry, the heart being the seat of the mischief. They note the progress of the sin the witnesses trace: first the heart is deceived, then the feet turn aside, then they serve and worship other gods; the apostasy begins within, in a heart led astray, before it shows in the act. They observe the lesson: that the great peril of a prosperous people is to forget the God who gives the plenty, and to put the creature, or their own ease, in his place; the heart must be kept with all diligence. They note that idolatry is the betrayal of the love God required. The verse thus warns of the deceived heart — the judgment of drought following in the next verse.
17:
On Deuteronomy 11:17 — “And then the LORD’s wrath be kindled against you, and he shut up the heaven, that there be no rain… and lest ye perish quickly from off the good land” — the witnesses mark the judgment of drought.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the LORD’s wrath kindled, the heaven shut up, the land yielding no fruit, and the people perishing quickly. The Reformed Gill: nothing more provoking to God than idolatry, contrary to his nature and glory. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: heaven is as a storehouse of rain (Job 38:22), whose doors God opens to give and shuts to withhold.

The verse threatens drought. Idolatry kindles God’s wrath; he shuts up heaven and they perish. The same rain that blesses is withheld in judgment.

So the witnesses see the warning sharpened by the threat of drought and ruin upon idolatry. The witnesses note the kindling of wrath: “then the LORD’s wrath be kindled against you” — Gill observes that nothing is more provoking to God than idolatry, being contrary to his very nature and being, and to his honour and glory; the turning to other gods would rouse his anger as nothing else. They mark the judgment that follows: “he shut up the heaven, that there be no rain, and that the land yield not her fruit” — the very blessing God had promised to obedience, the rain of heaven, he would withhold from the rebellious; Wesley draws out the figure that heaven is as a great storehouse of rain (Job 38:22), whose doors God opens when he gives and shuts when he withholds; the same hand that watered the land would, in wrath, make it barren. They observe the swiftness of the ruin threatened: “and lest ye perish quickly from off the good land which the LORD giveth you” — the land that depended wholly on the rain would, without it, soon fail, and the people with it; their hold on the good land was as quickly lost as it was given, if they forsook God. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that the gifts of God are held on the condition of obedience, and the same providence that blesses the faithful judges the rebellious; the rain is mercy or judgment as the people are true or false. They observe that idolatry brings drought to the soul as to the land. They note that God’s gifts withdrawn are his sorest judgments. The verse thus threatens drought — the charge to lay up his words following in the next verse.
18:
On Deuteronomy 11:18 — “Therefore shall ye lay up these my words in your heart and in your soul, and bind them for a sign upon your hand, that they may be as frontlets between your eyes” — the witnesses mark the laying up of God’s words in the heart.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott: the same injunctions as in Deuteronomy 6:6–9; the Jewish commentator remarks they would remember them in captivity, if not before. The Puritan Henry: Moses repeats the directions for keeping up religion. The Reformed Calvin: to lay up in the heart is to hide deeply in it. Wesley: let our hearts be filled with the word, our eyes fixed on it, our hands employed for it.

The verse charges the heart. Lay up God’s words in heart and soul, bind them as a sign. The word is to be kept always before them.

So the witnesses see Moses, against the danger of apostasy, charge the people to lay up God’s words deeply in their hearts. The witnesses note the inward keeping: “lay up these my words in your heart and in your soul” — Calvin explains that to lay up in or on the heart is to hide deeply in it, that the word should not be lightly or carelessly received and so soon let slip, but treasured in the inmost soul; Wesley bids us let our hearts be filled with the word of God, as a storehouse to be used on all occasions. They mark the outward signs commanded: “bind them for a sign upon your hand… as frontlets between your eyes” — the same injunctions, Clarke and Ellicott note, as were given at Deuteronomy 6:6–9; the word was to be bound on the hand (to guide the doing) and set between the eyes (to guide the seeing and the thinking), that it might govern the whole life; Wesley draws the three rules — the heart filled with the word, the eyes fixed on it, the hands employed for it. They observe Henry’s note that Moses here repeats the directions for the keeping up of religion among the people, knowing how soon the word is forgotten and how needful its constant remembrance. They note the sad remark of the Jewish commentator that the people would remember these words at last in their captivity, if not before — the very judgment that disobedience would bring. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the surest guard against the deceived heart and apostasy is the word of God hidden deep in the soul (Psalm 119:11). They note that what is laid up in the heart governs the life. The verse thus charges the heart — the teaching of children following (in the verses here passed over) and the promise of long days in the next.
21:
On Deuteronomy 11:21 — “That your days may be multiplied, and the days of your children… as the days of heaven upon the earth” — the witnesses mark the promise of long days as the days of heaven.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: it is written “to give them,” not “to give you”; hence the Jewish commentator finds the resurrection of the dead taught in the Law. The Reformed Gill: long life a desirable blessing, promised to those that keep the law. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “as the days of heaven” — as long as the heaven keeps its place and continues its influence upon earth.

The verse promises long days. Their days and their children’s shall be many, as the days of heaven. Length of days in the land rewards obedience.

So the witnesses see the keeping of God’s words in the heart rewarded with length of days for the people and their children. The witnesses note the promise: “that your days may be multiplied, and the days of your children” — Gill observes that long life was a desirable blessing, promised to those who keep the law; and here it is extended to their children after them, the obedience of the fathers blessing the generations. They mark the lofty figure: “as the days of heaven upon the earth” — Wesley explains it as meaning as long as the heaven keeps its place and continues its influence on the earth; that is, for a very long and enduring time, their days in the land should be many and lasting, while heaven endured. They observe the curious point Ellicott raises from the Jewish commentators: that the verse says the land was sworn “to give them” (the fathers), not “to give you”; from which the Rabbis argued that the resurrection of the dead is taught in the Law, since the fathers, to whom the land was promised, must rise to possess it — a remark, Ellicott says, worthy of a Christian commentator, and not far from our Lord’s own argument (Matthew 22:31–32). They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that obedience secures not only the present generation but their seed, and that the promise of long days, while given of the land, points beyond it to the everlasting life of the people of God. They observe that the blessing reaches to the children of the faithful. They note that God’s promises outlast the present life. The verse thus promises long days — the driving out of the nations following in the next verses.
22:
On Deuteronomy 11:22 — “For if ye shall diligently keep all these commandments… to love the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, and to cleave unto him” — the witnesses mark the call to love, walk in God’s ways, and cleave to him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “to walk in all his ways” — “He is compassionate, and thou shalt be compassionate; he showeth mercies, and thou shalt show mercies” (the Jewish comment, worthy of the New Testament); what follows shows the need of a mediator. The Reformed Gill: not a notional knowledge only, but to put them in practice.

The verse states the condition again. Love God, walk in his ways, cleave to him. Whole devotion is the term of the great promise.

So the witnesses see the condition of the great promise restated: to love God, to walk in all his ways, and to cleave to him. The witnesses note the threefold call: “to love the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways, and to cleave unto him” — love the spring, walking in his ways the course, and cleaving the steadfast adherence; the whole man given to God, in heart, in life, and in unwavering attachment. They mark Gill’s observation that the keeping required is not a bare notional or theoretical knowledge of the commandments, but the putting of them into practice; true keeping is doing. They observe the striking Jewish comment Ellicott records on “walking in all his ways”: that it means to imitate God — “he is compassionate, and thou shalt be compassionate; he showeth mercies, and thou shalt show mercies”; a saying, Ellicott marks, worthy of the New Testament, near to our Lord’s “be ye merciful, as your Father is merciful” (Luke 6:36); to walk in God’s ways is to be like him. They note the further point Ellicott draws, that what follows shows the need of a mediator: for such love and likeness to God are beyond man’s natural power, and point to the grace and the Mediator by whom alone they are attained. They observe the meaning of “cleave unto him”: to hold fast to God in unbroken loyalty, as a man cleaves to what he loves, not to be drawn away by any other. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God requires not a divided or partial service, but the whole heart cleaving to him, the life walking in his ways, the soul imitating his goodness. They observe that to cleave to God is the safety and the happiness of the soul. The verse thus restates the condition — the conquest promised in the next verses.
23:
On Deuteronomy 11:23 — “Then will the LORD drive out all these nations from before you, and ye shall possess greater nations and mightier than yourselves” — the witnesses mark the promise to drive out the nations.

The Reformed Gill: God would drive out all the seven nations of Canaan, by little and little, to make room for Israel to inherit, though they were greater and mightier than they.

The verse promises conquest. God will drive out nations greater than they. The land is won by God’s hand, not their strength.

So the witnesses see the reward of cleaving to God: he himself will drive out the nations of Canaan before his people. The witnesses note the promise: “then will the LORD drive out all these nations from before you” — Gill observes that God would drive out all the seven nations that then inhabited the land, and that (as elsewhere) by little and little, making room for Israel to inherit; the conquest would be God’s doing, the obedient people the instruments of his hand. They mark the greatness of the foe overcome: “ye shall possess greater nations and mightier than yourselves” — the nations to be dispossessed were larger and stronger than Israel; the victory was not to be by Israel’s own might, for they were the weaker, but by the power of God who fought for them. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that the conquest of the land hung on the people’s love and obedience (v. 22); if they cleaved to God, he would cleave to them, and overthrow nations far stronger than they; their strength lay not in themselves but in their God. They note the comfort and the condition together: that no enemy, however great, could stand before a people whom God favoured; yet the favour was promised to the obedient, and the same God who would drive out the nations for the faithful would (the next verses imply) let the land slip from the unfaithful. They observe that God overthrows the mighty for the sake of his obedient people. They note that the battle is the LORD’s. The verse thus promises the driving out of the nations — the extent of the land following in the next verse.
24:
On Deuteronomy 11:24 — “Every place whereon the soles of your feet shall tread shall be yours: from the wilderness and Lebanon, from the river, the river Euphrates, even unto the uttermost sea shall your coast be” — the witnesses mark the extent of the promised land.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: from the river Euphrates on the east to the uttermost sea, the Mediterranean, on the west; the promise fulfilled in Solomon’s time, despite Israel’s provocations. Charles Ellicott: repeated in Joshua 1:3. The Reformed Gill and Wesley: not absolutely every place, as the Rabbins fancy, but in the promised land, on condition of obedience.

The verse marks the bounds. From the wilderness to Lebanon, the Euphrates to the sea, all is theirs. The full extent of the land is promised.

So the witnesses see the wide extent of the land promised to the obedient people. The witnesses note the sweep of the promise: “every place whereon the soles of your feet shall tread shall be yours… from the wilderness and Lebanon, from the river Euphrates, even unto the uttermost sea” — Clarke marks the bounds: from the river Euphrates on the east to the uttermost (the Mediterranean) sea on the west, and from the southern wilderness to Lebanon in the north; the full compass of the land promised to Abraham (Genesis 15:18). They observe the fulfilment the witnesses note: that this promise, notwithstanding Israel’s many provocations, was made good in the time of Solomon, when his dominion reached from the Euphrates to the border of Egypt (1 Kings 4:21); God kept his word in its largest extent. They mark the caution Gill and Wesley give against a Jewish over-reading: that “every place the foot treads” is not to be taken absolutely, as the Rabbins fondly conceived (as if every land an Israelite set foot in were theirs), but is restrained by the following words to the promised land, and held on condition of their obedience. They note the figure of treading: that the land would be theirs as they went in and possessed it, foot by foot, by faith and obedience; what they trod in faith God gave. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that God’s gift was large and his promise sure, yet to be entered into by an obedient people; the inheritance was as wide as the promise and as sure as God’s word. They note that God’s promises are fulfilled to the faithful. The verse thus marks the bounds — the fear of Israel upon the nations following in the next verse.
25:
On Deuteronomy 11:25 — “There shall no man be able to stand before you: for the LORD your God shall lay the fear of you and the dread of you upon all the land that ye shall tread upon” — the witnesses mark the fear of Israel upon the nations.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: Rashi — “the fear on those near, the dread on those far off”; a far-reaching prophecy, “upon all the earth that ye shall tread.” The Reformed Gill: not a single man, nor any nation, should be able to stand before them.

The verse promises victory. None shall stand before them; God lays fear on the nations. The dread of God’s people goes before them.

So the witnesses see the promise of irresistible victory, God laying the fear of his people upon the nations. The witnesses note the assurance: “there shall no man be able to stand before you” — Gill observes that not a single man, however mighty (no Og, no son of Anak), nor any nation, should be able to withstand Israel; the obedient people would be invincible, not by their own strength but by God’s. They mark the means of the victory: “the LORD your God shall lay the fear of you and the dread of you upon all the land” — God would go before his people, striking terror into the hearts of their enemies, so that the nations would be unmanned and fall before them; the battle would be half won by the fear God sent ahead. They observe Ellicott’s note of Rashi’s distinction, “the fear upon those that are near, the dread upon those that are far off”; and that the promise is far-reaching, “upon all the land — or earth — that ye shall tread upon,” the dread of God’s people spreading wide. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that when God is with his people, no enemy can stand; he fights for them not only by their sword but by the terror he casts on their foes; the fear of God upon the nations is the secret of his people’s victories. They observe that the same fear that fell on Jericho (Joshua 2:9) is here promised. They note that God’s presence makes his people dreadful to their enemies. The verse thus promises victory — the blessing and curse set before them in the next verses.
26:
On Deuteronomy 11:26 — “Behold, I set before you this day a blessing and a curse” — the witnesses mark the blessing and the curse set before the people.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, “blessing and cursing” — the blessing if ye obey, the curse if ye do not. The Puritan Henry: Moses concludes his exhortation in a most affecting way. The Wesleyan-Arminian Adam Clarke: if God had not put it in their power to obey or disobey, had they not free will, the setting of blessing and curse before them would be absurd. The Reformed Calvin: he embraces the two at once — blessed if they keep the Law, cursed if they shake off its yoke.

The verse sets the choice. A blessing and a curse are set before them. Life and death hang on their obedience.

So the witnesses see Moses close his exhortation by setting before the people, in the most solemn manner, a blessing and a curse. The witnesses note the gravity of the moment: “Behold, I set before you this day a blessing and a curse” — Henry observes that Moses concludes his general exhortation to obedience in a most affecting way, laying the whole issue of life and death before them, as one would think must engage them forever to God. They mark the simple terms Ellicott gives: blessing and cursing — the blessing if they obey, the curse if they do not; the alternative is plain and the choice momentous. They observe the use Clarke makes of this setting of the choice: that if God had not put it in the people’s power either to obey or to disobey, if they had not had a free will over which they had authority, the setting of blessing and curse before them would be absurd and meaningless; the very offer supposes their responsibility and power to choose. They observe Calvin’s balancing word: that Moses here embraces the two at once — the people would be blessed if they earnestly applied themselves to keeping the Law, and cursed if they shook off its yoke and revelled in their lusts; the covenant holds out both, that the people might be moved by hope and warned by fear. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that God deals with men as responsible beings, setting good and evil, life and death before them, and pressing them to choose the good (Deuteronomy 30:19); the blessing and the curse are real, and the choosing theirs. They observe that the gospel too sets life and death before men. They note that the choice is solemn and the consequences eternal. The verse thus sets the choice — the blessing defined in the next verse.
27:
On Deuteronomy 11:27 — “A blessing, if ye obey the commandments of the LORD your God, which I command you this day” — the witnesses mark the blessing promised to obedience.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a blessing, if ye obey the commandments. The Reformed Gill: a blessing should come upon them, all temporal blessings — in body and estate, in their families, in city and field.

The verse defines the blessing. It comes upon obedience to God’s commandments. Obedience is the way of blessing.

So the witnesses see the blessing of the covenant defined: it comes upon obedience to God’s commandments. The witnesses note the condition: “a blessing, if ye obey the commandments of the LORD your God” — the blessing was not unconditional, but hung upon the people’s obeying the commandments God gave them that day; to obey was to be blessed. They mark the fulness of the blessing Gill describes: all temporal blessings the people stood in need of would come upon the obedient — they should be blessed in body and in estate, in their families, in their flocks and herds, in the city and in the field, going out and coming in; the whole of life would be under God’s favour (as the fuller list of Deuteronomy 28:3 sets out). They observe the plainness of the way the witnesses see: that God ties his blessing to obedience, not arbitrarily, but as the fit and ordered fruit of a right relation to him; the obedient walk in the way of blessing, and the blessing follows them. They note the encouragement in this: that God is ready and willing to bless his people, and asks only that they obey him; the blessing is held out as a motive to obedience. They observe the gospel pattern the witnesses see beneath the temporal promise: that obedience and blessing are joined in God’s ordering, and that the way of God’s commandments is the way of true and lasting good. They note that to obey is to enter into blessing. They observe that God’s commandments are not grievous, but the path to his favour. The verse thus defines the blessing — the curse following in the next verse.
28:
On Deuteronomy 11:28 — “And a curse, if ye will not obey the commandments… but turn aside out of the way… to go after other gods, which ye have not known” — the witnesses mark the curse upon disobedience and apostasy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a curse, if ye will not obey, but turn aside to go after other gods. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “which ye have not known” — gods you have no acquaintance with, nor experience of their power, wisdom, or goodness, as you have had of mine.

The verse defines the curse. It falls upon disobedience and the turning to other gods. Apostasy brings the curse.

So the witnesses see the curse of the covenant defined: it falls upon disobedience and the forsaking of God for idols. The witnesses note the condition: “a curse, if ye will not obey the commandments of the LORD your God” — as the blessing came upon obedience, so the curse upon refusing to obey; Gill notes the obverse of the blessing — the disobedient should be accursed in body and estate, in family, flock, and herd, in city and field, going out and coming in (as Deuteronomy 28:16 sets out at length). They mark the special form of the disobedience: “but turn aside out of the way… to go after other gods” — the curse is bound especially to apostasy, the leaving of God’s way to follow idols; the deepest disobedience is the forsaking of God for false gods. They observe Wesley’s pointed note on “which ye have not known”: that these other gods were ones the people had no acquaintance with, nor any experience of their power, wisdom, or goodness, as they had had of the LORD’s; the folly of apostasy is that men forsake the God they have proved for gods they have never known to do them any good. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that to turn from the known and tried God to unknown idols is both sin and madness, and brings the curse; the ingratitude and the folly are alike condemned. They observe that the curse is the just recompense of forsaking God. They note that God sets the curse before them not to destroy but to warn and deter. The verse thus defines the curse — the place of its proclaiming, at Gerizim and Ebal, following in the next verses.
29:
On Deuteronomy 11:29 — “Thou shalt put the blessing upon mount Gerizim, and the curse upon mount Ebal” — the witnesses mark the blessing and curse set on the two mountains.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the etymology of the names may cast light — Gerizim from a root “to cut.” Charles Ellicott: the Targum reads “those that bless” and “those that curse” (cf. Deuteronomy 27:12). The Reformed Calvin: God appointed a solemn rite. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: “put,” that is, speak or pronounce, or cause to be pronounced.

The verse appoints the place. The blessing on Gerizim, the curse on Ebal. The covenant is to be solemnly proclaimed.

So the witnesses see the blessing and the curse appointed to be solemnly proclaimed from two mountains when Israel entered the land. The witnesses note the command: “thou shalt put the blessing upon mount Gerizim, and the curse upon mount Ebal” — Wesley explains “put” as “speak or pronounce, or cause to be pronounced”; the blessings were to be solemnly proclaimed from or toward Gerizim, the curses from or toward Ebal, in a great public ceremony (set out at large in Deuteronomy 27:12 and fulfilled in Joshua 8:33). They mark Ellicott’s note that the Targum reads “those that bless” and “those that curse,” the tribes being divided, half on each mount, to answer Amen to the blessings and the curses. They observe Clarke’s remark on the names, that their etymology may cast some light, Gerizim coming from a root meaning “to cut”; the two mountains, standing over against each other with the valley of Shechem between, made a natural amphitheatre for so solemn a proclamation. They note the fitness the witnesses see in the rite: that the covenant, with its blessing and curse, was not only spoken by Moses but to be ratified by the whole nation in the land itself, the people themselves owning the terms with their Amen; the blessing and curse were set before their eyes in the two mountains, and sealed by their own mouths. They observe Calvin’s note that God appointed this solemn rite to impress the covenant deeply on the people. They note that the choice of life and death was to be publicly and solemnly set before them. The verse thus appoints the place — its location described in the next verse.
30:
On Deuteronomy 11:30 — “Are they not on the other side Jordan… in the champaign over against Gilgal, beside the plains of Moreh?” — the witnesses mark the location of the mountains by the oaks of Moreh.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “where the sun goeth down” — a memorable passage attesting the speaker east of Jordan; the sun seen setting in the gap between the two mountains. The Reformed Calvin: the interrogation affirms more vehemently that these mountains were in the land. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: beside the plains of Moreh, one of the first places Abram came to in Canaan.

The verse locates the mountains. They lie beyond Jordan, by the plains of Moreh. The place of the proclaiming is fixed.

So the witnesses see the two mountains located by familiar landmarks, west of Jordan, by the plains of Moreh. The witnesses note the description: “on the other side Jordan… in the champaign over against Gilgal, beside the plains” (or oaks) “of Moreh” — the mountains lay across the Jordan from where Moses then stood in the plains of Moab, in the land of Canaan proper, near Shechem. They mark Calvin’s point on the form of the question, “are they not…?”: that the interrogation affirms the more vehemently that these mountains were indeed in the land they were to possess; Moses appeals to the people’s knowledge of the place to confirm the certainty of their entering. They observe Ellicott’s note on the phrase “where the sun goeth down,” which he calls a memorable attestation of the true position of the speaker, east of Jordan; travellers have seen the sun set in the very gap between the two mountains, as one looks westward from that side. They mark the rich association Wesley draws out: that “the plains” (or oaks) “of Moreh” was one of the first places Abram came to in Canaan, where God appeared to him and promised the land to his seed (Genesis 12:6–7); so that in sending the people to hear the blessing and the curse at this very place, God led them to where the promise had first been given to their father. They note the fitness the witnesses see: that the covenant should be ratified at the place where it was first promised, the seed entering on the inheritance pledged to Abraham. They observe that God’s leadings are full of meaning. The verse thus locates the mountains — the charge to possess and obey following in the next verses.
31:
On Deuteronomy 11:31 — “For ye shall pass over Jordan to go in to possess the land which the LORD your God giveth you, and ye shall possess it, and dwell therein” — the witnesses mark the assurance of passing over to possess the land.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: in the place of Shechem, by the oak of Moreh, the LORD first appeared to Abram and said, “Unto thy seed will I give this land” (Genesis 12:7); the first recorded promise to the patriarch. The Reformed Gill: they were now near it, and by this assured they should pass over and take possession.

The verse assures the entry. They shall pass over Jordan, possess the land, and dwell there. The promise of possession is renewed.

So the witnesses see the people assured that they should pass over Jordan, possess the land, and dwell in it. The witnesses note the assurance: “ye shall pass over Jordan to go in to possess the land… and ye shall possess it, and dwell therein” — Gill observes that the people were now near the land, and by these words were assured that they should certainly cross the river, take possession, and settle in the inheritance God gave them; the entry was sure, founded on God’s gift. They mark the rich association Ellicott draws to the place named (Shechem, by the oak of Moreh): that it was there the LORD first appeared to Abram and said, “Unto thy seed will I give this land” (Genesis 12:7), the first recorded promise of the land to the patriarch; so the people, in going to this very spot to ratify the covenant, came to where the promise had first been spoken to their father, the seed now entering on what was pledged to Abraham. They observe the certainty the witnesses see: that what God had promised to Abraham, and now renewed to his seed, would surely be performed; the possession was as sure as God’s word, given to the obedient. They note the fitness of the assurance here, before the charge to obey that follows: that they might enter the land in confidence of God’s gift, yet hold it by obedience to his law. They observe that God’s promises are sure to those who keep his covenant. They note that the gift of the land and the duty of obedience go together. The verse thus assures the entry — the charge to observe and do following in the next verse.
32:
On Deuteronomy 11:32 — “And ye shall observe to do all the statutes and judgments which I set before you this day” — the witnesses mark the closing charge to do all the statutes.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: ye shall observe to do all the statutes and judgments which I set before you this day. The Reformed Gill: take notice of them, and heed unto them, so as to practise them; on their observance depended their continuance in the land.

The verse closes with the charge. Observe to do all the statutes and judgments. Possession is held by obedience.

So the witnesses see the discourse close, as it ran throughout, with the charge to observe and do all God’s statutes. The witnesses note the command: “ye shall observe to do all the statutes and judgments which I set before you this day” — Gill observes that the people were to take notice of the laws and give heed to them, not for knowledge only but so as to practise them; the observing was to issue in doing. They mark the breadth of it: “all the statutes and judgments” — not a chosen part, but the whole body of God’s laws set before them that day; the obedience was to be entire. They observe the connection the witnesses see with all that went before: that, having assured them of entering and possessing the land (v. 31), Moses presses upon them that their continuance and welfare in it depended on their keeping the law; the gift was God’s, but the holding of it was by obedience. They note the fitness of so closing the exhortation: that the whole discourse, with its rehearsal of God’s acts, its promise of rain and plenty, its warning of drought and curse, its setting of blessing and curse before them, comes to rest in this one charge — observe to do all that God commands. They observe the gospel echo the witnesses hear: that the hearing of God’s word is to issue in the doing of it, and the doer, not the hearer only, is blessed (James 1:22). They note that the chapter ends where it began, in the call to love and obey God. They observe that obedience is the sum of the whole. The verse thus closes with the charge — the discourse of the covenant and the law brought to its end.
